coredata.dc:title: Facile oxidation reaction to produce monolayered highly crystalline nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lawrence, Reece T.
Lu, Cheng
Croxall, Mark P.
Yu, Kevin
Cynthia Goh, M.
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) made by standard hydrothermal synthesis route were found to produce non-uniform nanostructures that exhibit poor crystallinity and unstable optical properties. In this study, a simple post synthesis oxidation step was shown to significantly improve the overall crystallinity and stability of the nanostructures via removal of reaction by-products and oxidation of reactive functional groups, generating oxidized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Ox-NGQDs). Through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) the Ox-NGQDs were found to be uniform monolayered nanostructures with lateral size of 20–25 nm. The appearance of G and D bands in the Raman spectrum of the Ox-NGQDs sample confirmed that the reaction by-products had been removed, and indicated that the Ox-NGQDs were composed of a highly ordered graphitic structure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated that oxidation of the surface functional groups took place. The Ox-NGQDs produced in this work gained a significant increase in visible absorbance, which is important in a wide range of potential light harvesting applications and uses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NGQDs
Ox-NGQDs
Quantum Dots
Hydrothermal
Bottom-up

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) TEM images of NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs; (C) HR-TEM image of Ox-NGQDs; (D) AFM image of Ox-NGQDs.
XRD pattern for NGQDs (lower) and Ox-NGQDs (upper).
Raman spectra of NGQDs (lower) and Ox-NGQDs (upper).
Full scan XPS spectra of NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs (A); and high resolution of C 1s (B), N 1s (C), O 1s (D) of NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs.
UV–Vis absorbance (solid line) and photoluminescence (dashed line) spectra of NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs.
Elemental Analysis of NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs.
C 1s, O 1s, N 1s binding energies and atomic percentages

head.ce:title: Facile oxidation reaction to produce monolayered highly crystalline nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Toronto, 184 College St, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3E4, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Toronto, 184 College St, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3E4, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Toronto, 80 St. George St, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3H6, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Toronto, 80 St. George St, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3H6, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Toronto, 80 St. George St, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3H6, Canada.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) made by standard hydrothermal synthesis route were found to produce non-uniform nanostructures that exhibit poor crystallinity and unstable optical properties. In this study, a simple post synthesis oxidation step was shown to significantly improve the overall crystallinity and stability of the nanostructures via removal of reaction by-products and oxidation of reactive functional groups, generating oxidized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Ox-NGQDs). Through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) the Ox-NGQDs were found to be uniform monolayered nanostructures with lateral size of 20–25 nm. The appearance of G and D bands in the Raman spectrum of the Ox-NGQDs sample confirmed that the reaction by-products had been removed, and indicated that the Ox-NGQDs were composed of a highly ordered graphitic structure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated that oxidation of the surface functional groups took place. The Ox-NGQDs produced in this work gained a significant increase in visible absorbance, which is important in a wide range of potential light harvesting applications and uses.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Oxidized Nitogen Doped Graphene Quantum Dots

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with outstanding chemical stability and low toxicity have attracted considerable interest for applications in biosensing, bioimaging, energy conversion, and photocatalysis [1–5]. GQDs consist of sp2 hybridized carbon of single-to-few layers and are confined laterally to diameters in the nano-sized regime. The restricted size endows beneficial properties beyond traditional graphene, such as unique edge functional groups, quantum sized effects, and a wide range of optical properties [6]. Both “top down” and “bottom up” methods have been implemented to obtain GQDs. “Top down” techniques include cutting large flakes of graphene oxide down via electrochemical [7] or chemical cutting [8], carving graphite using electron beam lithography [9], etc. However, these approaches suffer from low yields, poor size control and often require expensive equipment. “Bottom up” techniques utilize precursor molecules to build up the graphitic lattice structured quantum dots through various chemical processes such as: pyrolysis [10], stepwise organic synthesis [11], and carbonization [12] which allow for a more refined control of size, composition, and morphology.

GQDs have shown attractive electronic and optical properties including enhanced photoluminescence and increased active surface sites due to quantum size confinement and rich functional groups on the edge sites [13]. There is increasing interest to tailor the band structure of GQDs for optimized optical and electronic properties. Nitrogen doping was widely used to increase the electronic density of carbon materials for optimized electronic property and chemical reactivity [14–16]. Both theoretical and experimental results have indicated the introduction of the nitrogen atoms into the graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) can tailor the electronic properties with enhanced fluorescent emission depending on nitrogen bonding [16].

A variety of nitrogen configurations are formed during the nitrogen doping process, such as pyridinic nitrogen (pyridinic N), pyrrolic nitrogen (pyrrolic N), and graphitic nitrogen (graphitic N) [17]. The graphitic-N is sp2 hybridized N replacing a C atom in the graphitic lattice, which is within the planar structure. Pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N are nitrogen species at the edge or in the vacancies of the graphitic matrix. Graphitic nitrogen contributes its free pair of electrons to the π* molecular orbital, which leads to a strong n-type doping effect. Pyrrolic and pyridinic edge states contribute one and two electrons into the π orbital respectively, which confer a weak p-doping effect [18]. Different N configurations can alter the electronic structure and change the chemical reactivity of GQDs for different applications.

The most prevalent “bottom up” synthesis for NGQDs is to react a carbon source with a nitrogen source, such as: urea [15,16,19,20], diethylenetriamine [16], polyethyleneimine [18] etc. in a hydrothermal reaction. Intermolecular interactions at the elevated temperature and pressure of the hydrothermal conditions form uniform, water-soluble NGQDs with a high degree of size control. Qu et al. reported a method using citric acid and urea to obtain fluorescent NGQDs through hydrothermal reaction at 160 ˚C. The obtained NGQDs contained a graphitic core structure with rich surface functional groups including: carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl and amines that endow them with enhanced optical properties and a high solution stability. However, in this approach, the NGQDs prepared exhibited poor crystallinity and thermostability because of the abundant reactive surface functional groups, while also generating a significant amount of organic by-products that require extra purification [21]. This further hinders NGQDs utilization for commercial or scientific applications where structure and chemical uniformity are critical, especially in light harvesting and photocatalysis. Other approaches have been demonstrated in generating highly crystalline NGQDs, such as bottom-up methods utilizing single molecule precursors including: 2,3-diaminophenazine [30], dimethylformamide [31], or melamine [34]. However, they require elevated temperatures (320 to 1000 °C) and long reaction times (24 to 72 h). Furthermore, they generally can only alter the composition of the NGQDs by varying reaction conditions; by utilizing multiple precursors for the nitrogen or carbon we are able to achieve a higher level of control and optimization.

Here we present a simple yet controllable post hydrothermal oxidation technique utilizing hydrogen peroxide at ambient conditions that improves the overall crystallinity and purity of the NGQDs produced via hydrothermal reaction. Through a controlled post oxidation treatment with hydrogen peroxide, we show that the partially reacted organic by-products attached on the edge of the NGQDs are cleaved off, producing highly crystalline and uniform Ox-NGQDs with significant visible light absorption.

All chemicals are commercially available and were used without further purification. Citric acid monohydrate and urea were purchased from Bioshop Canada Inc. Hydrogen peroxide (Sigma-Aldrich) concentration was determined via titration prior to use.

Elemental analysis (EA) was taken using a CHNS Analyzer (Flash 2000, ThermoFisher Scientific) in which oxygen is inferred from the missing mass and the results reported represent the average of 3 samples. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were taken using the JEOL 2010F electron microscope equipped with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted using a Nanoscope III Multimode SPM (Bruker). Raman spectroscopy were taken with a dispersive Raman microscope (SENTERRA, Bruker). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were taken using a diffractometer (Miniflex 600, Rigaku) with Cu-Kα radiation in the range of 5-80˚ 2θ at room temperature. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted using the ESCALAB 250XI (ThermoFisher Scientific) in which samples were prepared under argon and transferred into the instrument via glovebox. Absorbance measurements were taken on a Tecan Infinite M1000 plate reader at room temperature.

NGQDs were prepared via hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and urea [15]. A 3:1 M ratio of urea (1.44 g) to citric acid (1.68 g) was dissolved in 40 ml of de-ionized (DI) water under vigorous stirring. The solution was transferred into a Teflon lined hydrothermal reaction vessel and placed in the oven at 160 ˚C for 20 hrs. The reacted solution was roto-evaporated to produce a NGQDs powder. The NGQDs were further purified by 20 ml anhydrous alcohol with centrifugation for 3 times. The final powder was dried at 70˚C overnight.

NGQDs obtained after the hydrothermal reaction were re-dispersed into DI water at a concentration of 0.6 g/ml. 5% hydrogen peroxide by weight (0.03 g/ml) of dry NGQDs was added to the solution under vigorous stirring. A visible colour change from yellow to violet and finally to dark brown was observed, and the reaction was left to complete over 24hrs with no further visible colour change. The solution was then dried at 70 ˚C to produce the dry oxidized NGQDs (Ox-NGQDs). The yield was found to be approximately 81%.

The hydrothermal reaction temperature of 160˚C was selected following the reported work [15,19]. A reaction time of less than 5 hrs produced a tar-like substance that could not be dried into a powder. We attribute this to the formation of organic by-products that were rich with hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyls, carboxylic acids, amines and amides that trap bound water, which cannot driven off during roto-evaporation. The large portion of organic by-products were not favourable for the formation of crystalline NGQDs which displayed a weak XRD signal with no defined graphene diffraction.

To minimize the formation of organic by-products, a 20-hrs reaction time was chosen; this allowed dry powder to be collected during the roto-evaporation drying process. However, the obtained NGQD powder was unstable and exhibited a colour change from yellow to dark violet within weeks of synthesis under normal ambient atmospheric exposure. A similar colour change in NGQDs was also reported by Li et al. who attributed the instability to edge organic groups [21]. The observed colour change occurred significantly quicker for NGQDs in an aqueous solution, and quicker still when the solution was under UV light illumination. The change in colour was accompanied by a decay in the photoluminescent signal as shown in Figure S2. A clear increase in broadband visible adsorption (450 nm – 750 nm) appeared after 3 days in solution and continued increasing over 28 days of observation (Fig S3).

The powder NGQDs could be stored in a desiccator or low humidity environment for upwards of one year without visible colour change. We also noticed the rate of colour change decreased when Ar gas was introduced into the NGQD solution. We suspect that the colour variation is due to the interaction of organic by-products with H2O and oxygen, indicating that oxidation was the cause of the change, which suggests instability in the as-formed NGQDs.

In order to control the stability and limit the organic by-products, hydrogen peroxide was introduced for controlled oxidization of the post hydrothermal NGQDs. With the addition of hydrogen peroxide into the NGQD solution, the colour changed from yellow though dark green to a dark brown, similar to the changes observed under ambient conditions discussed earlier. This reaction was carried out over a 20 hrs period until no further visible changes occurred. The solution was subsequently dried at 70˚C to produce dry and stable oxidized NGQDs (Ox-NGQDs). The Ox-NGQDs and NGQDs were subsequently analysed to determine the change in material properties via the controlled oxidation reaction.

Table 1 shows the elemental analysis (EA) results for both NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs. The predominant elements in both samples are carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and oxygen. The elemental analysis confirms that nitrogen has successfully been incorporated into both samples. After hydrogen peroxide treatment, the percentage of carbon increased from 35.50% to 40.01%, while the percentage of all the other elements decreased. Many of the hydrophilic organic by-products are likely cleaved off via the oxidation reaction with H2O2, which can account for the relative increase in carbon, and decrease in nitrogen and oxygen as indicated via the elemental analysis. This oxidation reaction could be a process similar to thermal carbonization, but more controlled and can be carried out under ambient temperature and pressure, which allows for a finer level of specificity over surface functional groups and particle size.

The organic by-product structures present in the un-oxidized NGQDs are clearly visible via TEM (Fig. 1 A), which appear to form a membrane-like structure with no distinct particles present. The membrane is no longer visible after hydrogen peroxide treatment, and distinct circular particles with an average diameter of 21.23 nm ± 3.59 nm and a defined lattice structure (Fig. 1B) are visible. The observed membrane is attributed to the organic by-product structures possibly generated by the incomplete reaction between urea and citric acid that link all NGQDs formed through carboxylic, carbonyl or amino functional groups [15,21,22].

The height of the Ox-NGQDs were measured via AFM, shown in Fig. 1D and Fig S4. The height profile of the Ox-NGQDs measured via AFM (Fig. 1D) is shown to be of plateau like structure, approximately 0.6 nm in height, suggesting that the Ox-NGQDs are of monolayer [8]. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images revealed that the Ox-NGQDs consist of a highly crystalline structure with the lattice spacing 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) graphene plane (Fig. 1C) [23].

The improvement in overall NGQD crystallinity post hydrogen peroxide treatment was further confirmed through powder XRD measurements. Fig. 2 shows the background corrected XRD spectra for both NGQDs (lower line) and Ox-NGQDs (upper line). The NGQDs exhibit several sharp diffraction peaks between 10 and 40°, which do not correspond to the individual spectra of either citric acid or urea (Fig S5). This further suggests that organic by-products are present. These peaks are absent from the Ox-NGQDs spectra, suggesting the intermediary structures have been removed by the hydrogen peroxide. The broad peak at 26° in the Ox-NGQDs corresponds to the (002) interlayer spacing of graphene (approximately 0.34 nm) [12,14] and confirms the formation of crystalline graphene quantum dot structures.

Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize the quality of the graphitic lattice present in both samples (Fig. 3 ). Peaks centred at approximately 1365 and 1570 cm−1 are attributed to the “D band” and “G band” respectively, where the G band corresponds to the in-plane optical phonon modes (sp2 hybridized) and the D band represents the phonon modes for defects (sp3 hybridized) present within the lattice [24]. The NGQDs exhibit no discernible peak at either G or D band position, possibly due to the organic by-products decorating the edge of the graphitic lattice, nullifying the signal from the graphitic portion of the NGQDs.

The Ox-NGQDs exhibit a significantly more defined Raman spectra, with a strong G band at 1570 cm−1 and a smaller D band at 1360 cm−1 being visible. The relative ratio of the D band to G band (ID/IG) is used to infer the relative number of defects present in graphitic samples. The Ox-NGQDs have a ratio of 0.57 which indicates that the graphene is of minimal defects compared with the other reported NGQDs (ID/IG = 0.9 Qu, D. et al. 2014, ID/IG = 0.7 Li, Yan, et al. 2011). This highlights the potential of a post oxidation treatment for generating highly crystalline nanomaterials generated via bottom-up synthesis techniques.

Fig. 4 shows the full survey scan and high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra of the NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs samples. The individual contributions within the high-resolution spectra are reported in Table 2 . The C 1s (Fig. 4B) binding energies are characterized by contributions from three regions: region I at ∼ 284.8 eV due to C = C/C-C [18], region II ∼ 287 eV due to C-N or C-O [18,25,26], and region III ∼ 288.6 eV, arising from C = O/C(=O)-N [27] and O-C = O [14,18]. The atomic ratios of the carbon assignments is also presented in Table 2 which shows the ratio of region III dropping from 34% to 27%. This suggests the cleavage of carbonamide/carbonyl moieties present in the sample by hydrogen peroxide, which are likely part of the organic by-products present on the edge of the NGQDs. Meanwhile, the atomic increase in region II indicates more heteroatoms (N, O) are inserted into structure, likely increasing the N-doping ratio of the lattice of the Ox-NGQDs.

The O 1s spectra (Fig. 4D) indicates the removal of carbonyl moieties post hydrogen peroxide treatment, consistent with what is observed in the C 1s spectra. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 4C) were deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energy at ∼ 399.4 eV, 400.2 eV, and 401.4 eV. The assignment of these peaks is shown in Table 2. Five kinds of nitrogen are expected in the obtained NGQDs samples, including pyridinic N at 399.4 eV, pyrrolic N (C-NH-C) and C-NH2 at 400.2 eV, and graphitic nitrogen and protonated amino group at 401.4 eV [19,20,27]. For the latter two pairs, their binding energy is so close that they cannot be resolved more accurately. Comparing the two samples, the pyrrolic contribution stays roughly the same, while the pyridinic atomic ratio increases from 24% (NGQDs) to 33% (Ox-NGQDs) after oxidation. This corresponds with a decreased contribution, from 35% (NGQDs) to 23% (Ox-NGQDs) of the graphitic / protonated amino moieties.

It is assumed that the graphitic nitrogen is not oxidized via hydrogen peroxide due to its aromatic stability, and the decrease is attributed to the removal of protonated amino groups which is consistent with the overall improvement in crystallinity shown from XRD and Raman. This is likely due to the hydrogen peroxide preferentially oxidizing the sp3 carbon related to these functional groups over the core sp2 graphitic structures, as previous studies have shown [32,36]. This was confirmed through FT-IR (see Fig. 5 ) in which the C = C stretch associated with the graphitic lattice is present in both samples (1550 cm−1) [33], but the C = N stretch of the graphitic nitrogen (1660 cm−1) [34] is only present in the Ox-NGQDs. The C = C stretch associated with the D band (1360 cm−1) [30] as indicated via the Raman spectra is also reduced for the Ox-NGQDs, further suggesting the hydrogen peroxide acts to remove the intermediates or defects present in the NGQDs.

Hydrogen peroxide treatment of NGQDs can successfully remove the functional groups and intermediate groups with rich functionalities, leading to more stable optical properties. Fig. 6 shows the UV–Vis absorbance and photoluminescence emission spectra for the NGQDs and Ox-NGQDs. The two absorption bands centred at ∼230 nm and ∼340 nm can be attributed to the σ-σ* and π-π* molecular transitions of the graphitic sp2 network [16,21]. The higher absorption for NGQDs without H2O2 treatment suggests that the intermediate products also have conjugated structures. The Ox-NGQDs show a broad absorption band ranging from 350 nm to 700 nm, which is consistent with their dark visual appearance. This large optical absorption range is attributed to the n-π* transition of the delocalized free electron pair on the nitrogen and oxygen [28,29], which contributes varying donor–acceptor properties to the conjugated network depending on bonding configuration. H2O2 treatment quenched the photoluminescent emission, as shown in Fig. 6. As other groups have suggested, the enhanced photoluminescence generated by NGQDs is likely due to nitrogen containing functional groups (amines, amides etc) which donate delocalized electrons to the graphitic structure [21,35]. Similarly, the removal of these functional groups via hydrogen peroxide as shown via XPS are likely responsible for the decreased photoluminescence in the Ox-NGQDs.

We showed that through an ambient oxidation reaction with hydrogen peroxide we can substantially increase the crystallinity and uniformity of hydrothermally produced NGQDs. Via oxidation reaction we modified the electronic structure of the NGQDs generating surface states rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-related functional groups. These functional groups via their non-bonding lone pairs significantly enhanced the Ox-NGQDs visible absorption which makes them ideal for light harvesting applications that require or are enhanced by visible light absorption.

Reece T. Lawrence: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Cheng Lu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mark P. Croxall: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Kevin Yu: Investigation. M. Cynthia Goh: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the funding received from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council (NSERC) of Canada, and the Ontario Graduate Scholarship awarded to R.L. (OGS).
